message:
feat(robot): extended wait conditions in capability registry, snapshot summary tests, beads metadata

Capabilities (capabilities.go):
- Wait command description updated to "Wait for pane-state or
  attention-feed conditions and return a machine-usable wake result"
- --wait-until lists all 17 supported conditions explicitly (including
  stalled, rate_limited, attention, action_required, mail_pending, etc.)
- --wait-transition clarified as pane-state-only
- New example: ntm --robot-wait=proj --wait-until=action_required

Docs (docs.go):
- Agent Control section: --robot-wait described as "Wait for pane
  state or attention-feed condition"
- New wait_for_attention example with --wait-until=action_required

Tests (capabilities_test.go):
- TestBuildCommandRegistryWaitCommandReflectsExtendedConditions:
  verifies 12 attention conditions listed, --wait-transition scope,
  action_required example
- TestDocsContentMentionsAttentionWait: verifies attention-feed
  references in Agent Control section

Tests (robot_helpers_pure_test.go):
- TestBuildSnapshotAttentionSummary_UsesReplayWindowAndEventActions:
  replay window boundaries, event NextActions propagation, follow-cursor
- TestBuildSnapshotAttentionSummary_UsesDigestWhenOnlyInterestingEventsExist:
  interesting-only feeds produce digest handoff and resync next steps

Beads (.beads/issues.jsonl):
- Minor issue status updates

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"br-7746h","title":"Attention Feed Phase 4b0: Build shared Go scenario harness, tmux fixtures, and artifact plumbing for transcript and e2e verification","description":"# Why this bead exists
Both the core attention-feed initiative and the overlay-feed integration branch now require Go-based end-to-end/transcript verification with rich artifacts. If each branch invents its own tmux fixture lifecycle, artifact pathing, timeout policy, and logging format, the result will be duplicated, inconsistent, and much harder to debug. This bead creates one shared Go verification harness so the later transcript/e2e beads can focus on scenarios instead of re-building plumbing.

# Scope
1. Build reusable Go helpers for tmux-backed scenario setup/teardown, including deterministic session naming, cleanup on failure, and explicit timeout handling.
2. Define one shared artifact strategy for transcript/e2e runs: directory layout, naming, timestamp format, retention/debug mode, and where stdout/stderr, cursor traces, rendered summaries, and transport captures should be written.
3. Provide reusable helpers for invoking ntm robot/CLI/network surfaces, capturing structured output, and annotating artifact timelines with scenario steps.
4. Provide reusable assertion/logging helpers for cursor handoff, wake reasons, degraded markers, focus targets, and other operator-loop invariants that multiple e2e beads will need.
5. Keep the harness implemented in Go and suitable for use from `e2e/` and integration-style tests; do not rely on shell-script runners as the canonical infrastructure.
6. Make the harness neutral enough that both the core attention-feed transcript bead and the overlay-feed transcript bead can reuse it without forking conventions.

# Design considerations
- The harness should reduce duplication, not become a mini framework that obscures what each scenario is doing.
- Cleanup must be robust enough that failed tmux-based tests do not leave session clutter behind.
- Artifact layout should optimize post-failure diagnosis first, not just “green test” terseness.
- Shared helpers must preserve enough raw detail that later failures can still be understood without stepping through the harness internals.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Unit tests for artifact path generation, timestamp formatting, retention/debug behavior, and cleanup helpers.
- Tests for helper behavior when tmux setup fails, command execution fails, or cleanup itself encounters missing resources.
- Example harness-driven smoke scenarios that prove artifacts are emitted in the documented structure.
- Detailed `t.Logf` output in harness tests showing artifact root, session IDs, cleanup decisions, and retained artifact locations when assertions fail.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] A reusable Go scenario/artifact harness exists for transcript/e2e work.
- [ ] Both the core attention-feed and overlay-feed e2e/transcript beads can depend on it instead of inventing separate plumbing.
- [ ] Artifact layout, retention/debug mode, and cleanup behavior are explicit and tested.
- [ ] The harness improves diagnosability without forcing shell-script orchestration into the repo.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":180,"created_at":"2026-03-21T01:23:46.362083521Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T02:43:08.191993090Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T02:43:08.191497012Z","close_reason":"Implemented shared Go scenario harness with: ArtifactManager for standardized artifact layout, ScenarioHarness for tmux-backed e2e, RobotResult with JSON helpers, CursorTraceEntry for cursor tracking, assertion helpers. All unit tests passing.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","e2e","harness","overlay","tests","verification"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-7746h","depends_on_id":"br-aa0nj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:25:31.177950401Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-7dnb7","title":"Phase 3 VERIFY: Run Full TUI Inspector Suite — Structural Refactor Gate","description":"# Phase 3 Verification Gate

## Purpose
After all Phase 3 tasks complete, verify no regressions.

## Mandatory Steps

### 1. Build verification
  go build ./cmd/ntm 2>&1 | tee /tmp/gate3_build.log
  go vet ./internal/tui/... 2>&1 | tee /tmp/gate3_vet.log

### 2. Unit test regression check
  go test -v -count=1 -short ./internal/tui/... 2>&1 | tee /tmp/gate3_tests.log
  # Count passing tests
  PASS_COUNT=$(grep -c '^--- PASS' /tmp/gate3_tests.log)
  BASELINE_COUNT=$(grep -c '^--- PASS' /tmp/tui_test_baseline.log)
  echo "Tests: phase3=$PASS_COUNT baseline=$BASELINE_COUNT"
  # PASS_COUNT must be >= BASELINE_COUNT (may be higher due to new tests)
  if [ "$PASS_COUNT" -lt "$BASELINE_COUNT" ]; then
      echo "ERROR: Test count regression! $PASS_COUNT < $BASELINE_COUNT"
      exit 1
  fi

### 3. VHS capture suite
  bash /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/run_ntm_tui_suite.sh \
    --profiles ntm-dashboard-empty,ntm-dashboard-help,ntm-dashboard-navigate,ntm-dashboard-wide,ntm-palette \
    --suite-name "phase3_post_upgrade"

### 4. Snapshot comparison
  For each profile, compare phase3 vs previous phase snapshots.
  Log any visual differences.

### 5. Log summary
  Create /tmp/gate3_summary.json:
  {
    "phase": 3,
    "build": "pass|fail",
    "vet": "pass|fail",
    "test_count": N,
    "test_baseline": N,
    "test_regression": false,
    "vhs_profiles_pass": 5,
    "vhs_profiles_total": 5,
    "visual_regressions": []
  }

## Logging
- All commands log to /tmp/gate3_*.log
- Summary JSON at /tmp/gate3_summary.json
- VHS suite outputs at /tmp/tui_inspector/suites/phase3_post_upgrade/

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] go build succeeds
- [ ] go vet clean (no warnings)
- [ ] Test count >= baseline (no regression)
- [ ] All 5 VHS profiles pass
- [ ] No visual regressions vs previous phase
- [ ] Summary JSON created with all metrics
- [ ] All logs archived

## Gate Policy
If ANY check fails, investigate and fix before proceeding to next phase.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":15,"created_at":"2026-03-20T22:29:53.016835262Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T00:24:37.114325421Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["gate","phase-3","tui","verification"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-7dnb7","depends_on_id":"br-obaln","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-20T22:33:54.109045610Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-7dnb7","depends_on_id":"br-phdna","type":"related","created_at":"2026-03-20T22:34:54.992984241Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-7dnb7","depends_on_id":"br-zqcqd","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-20T22:33:55.912758959Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-7h8it","title":"Attention Feed Phase 5: Validate the end-to-end operator loop and enforce rollout guardrails","description":"# Why this bead exists
The final question is not merely "did the code compile?" but "did ntm become a more agent-intuitive nervous system without drifting into a fake coordinator, and do the human-facing summary surfaces agree with that reality?" This bead is the capstone validation and rollout gate for the attention-feed initiative.

# Scope
1. Validate the implemented operator loop end to end:
   - cold start with snapshot
   - wait or attention against a known cursor
   - wake on meaningful change
   - inspect raw events only when needed
   - resync cleanly when replay is no longer possible
2. Confirm CLI, HTTP, SSE, and WebSocket parity where promised.
3. Confirm the discoverability surfaces (`--robot-help`, `--robot-capabilities`) accurately reflect reality.
4. Confirm dashboard and terse output reflect the same frontier as the operator-facing feed.
5. Confirm low-noise operator mode and high-fidelity debug mode both behave as intended.
6. Confirm the feature did not smuggle in a planner, second task graph, or hidden coordinator state.
7. Confirm both the deterministic unit/integration suite and the transcript-style e2e suite actually make failures diagnosable.
8. Record rollout guardrails in the implementation/docs/help output so future contributors understand the intended boundary of the feature.
9. Treat overlay-specific follow-on integration as separate work unless those beads are explicitly pulled into scope later; this gate is for the core attention-feed initiative.

# Validation checklist
- [ ] `go build ./cmd/ntm`
- [ ] `go test -short ./...`
- [ ] `go test -v ./e2e/...`
- [ ] relevant targeted package tests pass
- [ ] transcript-style e2e coverage for the new operator loop passes
- [ ] cursor expiry / resync behavior is clear and non-misleading
- [ ] operator loop feels simpler than repeated snapshot polling
- [ ] no schema drift between the promised contract and actual outputs
- [ ] no accidental architectural overreach into planning/coordinating logic
- [ ] help/capabilities/dashboard/terse output agree with the implemented command set
- [ ] operator/debug profile behavior is coherent and documented
- [ ] unit/integration logs and e2e artifacts are detailed enough to debug failures after the fact

# Artifact expectations
Closing this bead should leave behind a concise verification record identifying what commands/tests were run and where the most useful transcript or log artifacts can be found.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":120,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:22.027176806Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T01:20:55.772387457Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","gate","phase-5","robot","verification"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-7h8it","depends_on_id":"br-9bmtl","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:30.518584145Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-7h8it","depends_on_id":"br-auag6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:30.534838247Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-7h8it","depends_on_id":"br-mamo3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:46.125456143Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-7h8it","depends_on_id":"br-rkh17","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:45.685713666Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"br-7kigk","title":"Attention Feed Phase 3c: Evolve --robot-wait into a condition-driven attention gate","description":"# Why this bead exists
`internal/robot/wait.go` already offers useful waiting behavior, but it is currently narrower and more polling-oriented than what an operator LLM needs. This bead evolves wait into a condition-driven attention gate that can sleep efficiently until specific kinds of change occur.

# Mandatory condition set
Existing conditions to preserve:
- `idle`
- `complete`
- `generating`
- `healthy`

New attention-oriented conditions to add:
- `attention`
- `action_required`
- `mail_pending`
- `mail_ack_required`
- `stalled`
- `rate_limited`
- `context_hot`
- `reservation_conflict`
- `file_conflict`
- `session_changed`
- `pane_changed`
- `bead_orphaned` (only if implemented honestly)

# Scope
1. Add named conditions and keep them enumerable/documented.
2. Add `--since-cursor` support so waiters can bind themselves to a known observation frontier.
3. Preserve sensible timeout, transition, and error semantics.
4. Return a machine-usable wake payload that includes the matched condition, relevant cursor boundary/hand-off information, and enough detail for a caller to move into digest/events follow-up without guessing.
5. Ensure wait decisions consume the same normalized feed/signal pipeline rather than duplicating state logic.
6. Explicitly reject a general expression language unless a clear need emerges later.
7. Make honest degradation explicit for conditions that are partially observable or intentionally unsupported in v1.

# Guardrails
- Keep the interface teachable to an LLM.
- Avoid condition semantics that depend on undocumented background polling behavior.
- If a condition cannot be implemented honestly, narrow it rather than shipping a misleading version.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Synthetic feed tests for each new condition.
- Timeout tests and transition-boundary tests.
- Tests for wake payload shape, matched-condition reporting, and cursor handoff semantics.
- Tests for honest degradation when a condition is unavailable or only partially observable.
- Scenario transcripts for wait wakeups and timeouts using snapshot-provided cursors.
- Detailed logs in tests for matched condition, timeout path, observed cursor range, returned wake payload, and ignored competing signals.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Existing wait functionality is preserved.
- [ ] The new condition set is available, documented, and consistent with the feed taxonomy.
- [ ] `--since-cursor` works as a real boundary, not a cosmetic flag.
- [ ] Wait responses expose machine-usable wake payloads and cursor handoff semantics.
- [ ] Timeouts and error payloads remain machine-usable.
- [ ] Condition behavior is covered by transcript-style scenarios and detailed logs.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":180,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:21.152265573Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:45:18.657173230Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","phase-3","robot","wait"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-7kigk","depends_on_id":"br-04qv0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:25.486200475Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-7kigk","depends_on_id":"br-3qs46","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:41.750052444Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-7kigk","depends_on_id":"br-89wvt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:43.271960296Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-7kigk","depends_on_id":"br-fsoxa","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:25.820885564Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-7kigk","depends_on_id":"br-vdfjr","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:41.383112878Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"br-7v4ns","title":"Attention Feed Phase 1b: Map existing bus/tracker/session signals into one normalized feed service","description":"# Why this bead exists
A journal alone is not useful if each producer speaks a different dialect. ntm already emits data from several places: event bus notifications, tracker state changes, session/pane lifecycle transitions, alerts, activity monitors, and other robot helpers. This bead makes those sources legible to a robot client through one internal mapping/query layer.

# Scope
1. Map existing tracker change types and bus events into the normalized envelope.
2. Decide which fields should be filled for each source: category, type, summary, details, source, and candidate `next_actions`.
3. Route session and pane lifecycle events through the same path used for replay and live subscription.
4. Create one shared query/subscription service so `--robot-events`, `--robot-digest`, wait conditions, and server streaming all consume the same underlying feed.
5. Keep raw output out of the high-level envelope unless it is genuinely required; the feed should tell an operator what changed, not dump pane text continuously.
6. Document unmapped or intentionally suppressed source events in code/tests so future contributors understand what was omitted and why.

# Design considerations
- Summary strings should be compact but concrete, because they are what an LLM will read most often.
- `details` should hold structured fields that later commands can use mechanically.
- `next_actions` must never point at imaginary commands; they should reference real robot-mode follow-ups such as `--robot-tail`, `--robot-context`, `--robot-status`, or the new feed surfaces.
- Avoid duplicating mapping logic in both CLI and server code; parity depends on one source of truth.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Mapping tests for tracker changes, bus events, session creation/removal, pane creation/removal, alerts, and activity changes.
- Tests that validate `next_actions` only reference real supported surfaces.
- Fixture coverage table that lists each input event family and the expected normalized output shape.
- Debug logging in tests for mapping misses or intentionally dropped events.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Existing ntm signals are representable through one normalized mapping path.
- [ ] Replay and live subscription go through the same mapping logic.
- [ ] Event summaries/details are specific enough to guide follow-up inspection without requiring raw logs.
- [ ] The implementation does not fork into separate CLI-only and server-only schemas.
- [ ] Unmapped or intentionally omitted signals are explicit rather than accidental.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":240,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:19.637988898Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T03:11:04.602754573Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T03:11:04.602174267Z","close_reason":"Added shared AttentionFeed publish helpers for tracker/logged/bus sources, live bus subscription, and mapping/service tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","events","mapping","phase-1","robot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-7v4ns","depends_on_id":"br-xmmaj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:22.564595243Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-7zdzv","title":"PHASE 0: Capture TUI Baselines via VHS Inspector","description":"# Phase 0: Capture TUI Baselines

## Why This Must Come First
Every subsequent TUI change needs a visual reference point. Without baselines, we cannot
objectively verify that upgrades improve the UI without introducing regressions. The TUI
Inspector infrastructure already exists — we just need to run it and store the artifacts.

## What To Do

### Step 1: Run doctor check
  bash /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/doctor_tui_inspector.sh
Verify: vhs, ttyd, ffmpeg all present and functional. Log the output.

### Step 2: Build NTM binary
  go build -o /tmp/ntm_baseline ./cmd/ntm
Record the binary size and build time.

### Step 3: Capture all 5 profiles
  bash /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/run_ntm_tui_suite.sh \
    --profiles ntm-dashboard-empty,ntm-dashboard-help,ntm-dashboard-navigate,ntm-dashboard-wide,ntm-palette \
    --suite-name "baseline_pre_upgrade"
Log: each profile's exit code, video duration, snapshot status.

### Step 4: Run native VHS tapes
  cd /data/projects/ntm/testdata/vhs && for tape in *.tape; do vhs "$tape" 2>&1 | tee /tmp/vhs_${tape%.tape}.log; done

### Step 5: Run existing TUI unit tests as baseline
  go test -v -short ./internal/tui/... 2>&1 | tee /tmp/tui_tests_baseline.log
Record: test count, pass/fail, duration. This is the baseline test count we must not regress.

### Step 6: Archive baseline artifacts
Store in /tmp/tui_inspector/suites/baseline_pre_upgrade/
Create a manifest: baseline_manifest.json with binary size, test count, capture metadata.

## Unit Test Baseline
Run and record all existing tests:
  go test -v -count=1 -short ./internal/tui/... 2>&1 | tee /tmp/tui_test_baseline.log
  go test -v -count=1 -short ./internal/cli/... 2>&1 | tee /tmp/cli_test_baseline.log
Count passing tests. This number MUST NOT decrease in any subsequent phase.

## Logging
- Log doctor check output to /tmp/tui_inspector/baseline_doctor.log
- Log each VHS capture stdout/stderr to /tmp/tui_inspector/baseline_capture_*.log
- Log test run output to /tmp/tui_test_baseline.log
- Create /tmp/tui_inspector/baseline_summary.json with:
  { "binary_size": N, "test_count": N, "test_pass": N, "capture_count": 5, "all_pass": bool }

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 5 TUI Inspector profiles capture successfully (exit code 0 in run_meta.json)
- All 5 snapshots extracted (snapshot_exists: true in each run_meta.json)
- Suite manifest shows 5/5 passed
- All existing TUI unit tests pass
- Baseline artifacts archived and accessible
- baseline_summary.json created with all metrics

## Files Involved
- /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/capture_ntm_tui.sh
- /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/run_ntm_tui_suite.sh
- /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/doctor_tui_inspector.sh
- /dp/tui_inspector/profiles/ntm-*.env (5 profiles)
- /data/projects/ntm/testdata/vhs/*.tape (5 tapes)

 {"id":"br-815ez","title":"Attention Feed Phase 3a: Build the digest engine that turns feed noise into token-efficient deltas","description":"# Why this bead exists
A frontier LLM does not usually want the raw event stream first. Most of the time it wants a compact answer to "what changed that I should care about?" This bead builds the digest engine that turns feed noise into token-efficient deltas while preserving the ability to drill into raw events when needed.

# Scope
1. Coalesce recent feed activity into a compact digest grouped by `actionable`, `interesting`, `background`, and suppressed counts.
2. Generate a stable summary string that is cheap for an operator LLM to consume.
3. Preserve cursor boundaries so the digest can be chained cleanly in a long-running tending loop.
4. Retain event references/details needed for targeted follow-up rather than forcing a fresh snapshot.
5. Avoid aggressive magic. Coalescing should be rule-based and testable, not mysterious.
6. Establish explicit suppression rules for repetitive output churn, lifecycle noise, and redundant alert spam so later changes do not quietly make digests unusably noisy.

# Design considerations
- Repeated passive output should usually be suppressed, not surfaced one-by-one.
- Important coordination or stall signals must survive coalescing.
- The digest should remain predictable enough that agents can build habits around it.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Tests for suppression of noisy output bursts.
- Tests that important signals survive coalescing.
- Golden tests for summary stability and bucket assignment.
- Benchmarks or stress tests for digesting large bursts without pathological allocations.
- Debug logging in tests that shows which source events were surfaced, coalesced, or suppressed and why.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The digest separates action-required changes from passive background churn.
- [ ] Summary strings remain compact but concrete.
- [ ] Suppression/coalescing rules are deterministic and testable.
- [ ] Cursor boundaries remain explicit.

 {"id":"br-obaln","title":"Phase 3a: Decompose dashboard.go (7017 LOC) into Logical Sub-Files","description":"# Decompose dashboard.go (7017 LOC) into Logical Sub-Files

## Background
7,017-line monolith — classic anti-pattern. All files stay in package dashboard.
ZERO LOGIC CHANGE — every function moves verbatim.

## Estimate: 120 minutes
This is a mechanical file-split. Each step is: cut code, paste to new file, add imports,
run go build. The Go compiler catches issues instantly. 7017 LOC ÷ ~800 LOC/file = ~9 files.
At ~12 min per file (cut, paste, fix imports, build) = ~108 min. Rounded to 120m.

## Target File Structure
  dashboard/
  ├── model.go         (~400)  Model struct, New(), constants, PostQuitAction
  ├── messages.go      (~200)  All custom message types
  ├── init_cmd.go      (~300)  Init(), initial fetch commands
  ├── update.go        (~1400) Update(), keyboard handling
  ├── update_data.go   (~1100) Data update handlers (accept*Msg)
  ├── view.go          (~800)  View(), header, footer
  ├── view_panels.go   (~1500) Content section, panel grid
  ├── helpers.go       (~800)  Formatting, utilities
  ├── run.go           (~220)  Run(), RunPopup(), RunWithOptions()

## AGENTS.md Compliance: No File Deletion
Per AGENTS.md RULE NUMBER 1: "NEVER delete a file without express permission."
After all code is extracted, dashboard.go should be reduced to a package-level comment:
  // Package dashboard — see model.go, update.go, view.go for implementation.
  // This file intentionally left as a redirect after decomposition.
  package dashboard
Do NOT delete dashboard.go without explicit user permission.

## CRITICAL: Zero Logic Changes
Every function moves verbatim. No renaming, restructuring, or improvements.
git diff should show only file additions and dashboard.go deletions.

## Implementation Steps
1. Create model.go → move Model struct, New(), constants → go build
2. Create messages.go → move all *Msg types → go build
3. Create init_cmd.go → move Init() → go build
4. Continue for each file (build after each move)
5. Reduce dashboard.go to redirect comment
6. go test -short ./internal/tui/dashboard/...

## Unit Tests (verify no regression)

### Pre/post test comparison protocol
  # Before:
  go test -v -count=1 ./internal/tui/dashboard/... > /tmp/dash_before.log 2>&1
  # After:
  go test -v -count=1 ./internal/tui/dashboard/... > /tmp/dash_after.log 2>&1
  # Compare (only timestamps should differ):
  diff <(grep '^---' /tmp/dash_before.log) <(grep '^---' /tmp/dash_after.log)

### New structural test: internal/tui/dashboard/structure_test.go
  func TestModelStructExists(t *testing.T) { var m Model; _ = m }
  func TestNewReturnsValidModel(t *testing.T) {
      m := New("test", "/tmp/test")
      if m.session != "test" { t.Error("session not set") }
  }
  func TestRunFunctionExists(t *testing.T) {
      var f func(string, string) (*PostQuitAction, error) = Run
      _ = f
  }

## E2E / VHS Verification
VHS captures MUST be pixel-identical to Phase 2 baselines.
Run all 5 profiles. Any visual difference = bug in the split.

## Logging
- No runtime logging changes (structural refactor only)
- Log decomposition completion: file count, LOC per file

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] dashboard.go reduced to redirect comment (not deleted)
- [ ] All code in logical sub-files (9 new files)
- [ ] go build ./cmd/ntm succeeds
- [ ] go vet ./internal/tui/dashboard/... clean
- [ ] All existing tests pass with identical results
- [ ] structure_test.go passes
- [ ] VHS captures pixel-identical to Phase 2
- [ ] No import cycles

## Files to Create
model.go, messages.go, init_cmd.go, update.go, update_data.go, view.go,
view_panels.go, helpers.go, run.go

Structural verification tests go in existing helpers_test.go — no new test file.
(AGENTS.md: no file proliferation)

 {"id":"br-ofwdn","title":"Phase 1c: Replace Custom Spinner with bubbles/spinner","description":"# Replace Custom Spinner with bubbles/spinner

## Background
internal/tui/components/spinner.go (130 LOC) defines a custom Spinner struct (NOT SpinnerModel)
with Init(), Update(), View(), and TickCmd() methods. It has frame sets, color, gradient support,
and respects AnimationsEnabled().

## ACTUAL Current API (verified from source)
  type Spinner struct {
      Style          SpinnerStyle
      Color          lipgloss.Color
      Frame          int
      FPS            time.Duration
      Label          string
      Gradient       bool
      GradientColors []string
  }
  func NewSpinner() Spinner
  func (s Spinner) Init() tea.Cmd
  func (s Spinner) Update(msg tea.Msg) (Spinner, tea.Cmd)
  func (s Spinner) View() string
  func (s Spinner) TickCmd() tea.Cmd

Used in: dashboard.go, layout.go, components_test.go
AnimationsEnabled() checked at: spinner.go lines 74, 84, 119

## What bubbles/spinner Provides
  type Model struct { Spinner Spinner; Style lipgloss.Style }
  func New(opts ...Option) Model
  func (m Model) Update(msg tea.Msg) (Model, tea.Cmd)
  func (m Model) View() string
  func (m Model) Tick() tea.Cmd  // returns TickMsg

## Implementation Plan — PRESERVE EXACT API SURFACE

### 1. Keep Spinner struct, change internals
The Spinner struct must keep its EXACT field names and method signatures because dashboard.go
and layout.go reference them directly. Internal implementation changes to delegate to
bubbles/spinner.

### 2. Key change: store bubbles spinner.Model internally
  type Spinner struct {
      inner          bspinner.Model  // bubbles/spinner
      Style          SpinnerStyle    // preserved field
      Color          lipgloss.Color  // preserved field
      Label          string          // preserved field
      Gradient       bool            // preserved field
      GradientColors []string        // preserved field
      // Frame and FPS no longer needed — bubbles handles internally
  }

### 3. Map SpinnerStyle to bubbles presets
  func mapStyle(s SpinnerStyle) bspinner.Spinner {
      switch s {
      case SpinnerDots: return bspinner.Dot
      case SpinnerLine: return bspinner.Line
      // ... etc
      }
  }

### 4. View() delegates to inner.View() with gradient overlay
  func (s Spinner) View() string {
      if !AnimationsEnabled() { return staticChar(s.Style) }
      base := s.inner.View()
      if s.Gradient { return applyGradient(base, s.GradientColors) }
      return base
  }

### 5. Update() delegates to inner.Update()
### 6. TickCmd() wraps inner.Tick()

## Unit Tests (REQUIRED)

### Expand: internal/tui/components/components_test.go (spinner section)
  func TestSpinnerAllStyles(t *testing.T) {
      // Verify NewSpinner() produces valid spinner for each style
      for _, style := range allSpinnerStyles() {
          s := NewSpinner()
          s.Style = style
          view := s.View()
          if view == "" { t.Errorf("style %d empty view", style) }
      }
  }

  func TestSpinnerReducedMotion(t *testing.T) {
      t.Setenv("NTM_ANIMATIONS", "0")
      s := NewSpinner()
      v1 := s.View()
      s, _ = s.Update(s.TickCmd())
      v2 := s.View()
      // Reduced motion: view should not change between ticks
  }

  func TestSpinnerGradient(t *testing.T) {
      s := NewSpinner()
      s.Gradient = true
      s.GradientColors = []string{"#ff0000", "#00ff00"}
      view := s.View()
      if view == "" { t.Error("gradient spinner empty") }
  }

  func TestSpinnerTickCmd(t *testing.T) {
      s := NewSpinner()
      cmd := s.TickCmd()
      if cmd == nil { t.Error("TickCmd should return non-nil") }
  }

  func BenchmarkSpinnerView(b *testing.B) {
      s := NewSpinner()
      b.ResetTimer()
      for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ { _ = s.View() }
  }

## E2E / VHS Verification
Spinners appear in dashboard loading states. Run ntm-dashboard-empty capture.

## Logging
- Debug log style mapping: "spinner: style %d -> bubbles %v"
- Debug log when AnimationsEnabled() returns false

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Spinner struct keeps exact same public API (fields + methods)
- [ ] All existing code in dashboard.go/layout.go compiles without changes
- [ ] All SpinnerStyle values map to valid bubbles presets
- [ ] Gradient overlay still works
- [ ] AnimationsEnabled() check preserved
- [ ] All existing spinner tests in components_test.go pass
- [ ] New tests pass
- [ ] VHS captures show correct spinner animation

## Files to Modify
- internal/tui/components/spinner.go (rewrite internals, keep API)

## Executive Summary
NTM's TUI (49,213 LOC) is a sophisticated Bubble Tea application with 22 dashboard panels,
3 Catppuccin themes, responsive 5-tier layout, and custom animations. However, it dramatically
underutilizes the Charmbracelet ecosystem it depends on. The `harmonica` spring physics library
is in go.mod with ZERO imports. `bubbles/table`, `bubbles/viewport`, `bubbles/progress`,
`bubbles/spinner`, `bubbles/help`, and `bubbles/list` are all available but replaced by
hand-rolled alternatives. Mouse support is never enabled. The `huh` forms library is absent.

This epic systematically upgrades the TUI to leverage these battle-tested components, adds
spring-based physics animations via Harmonica, enables mouse interaction, and decomposes the
7,017-line dashboard.go monolith — all verified at every step via the TUI Inspector VHS
capture-and-compare loop.

## Architecture Context
- Entry: `cmd/ntm/main.go` → `cli.Execute()` → Cobra commands
- Dashboard: `internal/tui/dashboard/dashboard.go` (7,017 LOC monolith)
- Components: `internal/tui/components/` (15 files, ~3,300 LOC)
- Styling: `internal/tui/styles/` + `internal/tui/theme/` (~3,000 LOC)
- Layout: `internal/tui/layout/layout.go` (5 responsive tiers with hysteresis)
- Panels: `internal/tui/dashboard/panels/` (22 implementations)
- TUI programs: dashboard, palette (cli/palette.go), tutorial (cli/tutorial.go), init_wizard

## Key Dependencies Already in go.mod
- charmbracelet/bubbletea v1.3.10
- charmbracelet/bubbles v1.0.0 (table, list, viewport, progress, spinner, help — all unused)
- charmbracelet/lipgloss v1.1.1-0.20250404203927-76690c660834
- charmbracelet/harmonica v0.2.0 (ZERO imports anywhere)
- charmbracelet/glamour v0.10.0
- charmbracelet/x/ansi v0.11.6

## Verification Infrastructure
- VHS installed at /home/ubuntu/go/bin/vhs
- 5 NTM-specific TUI Inspector profiles: ntm-dashboard-empty, ntm-dashboard-help,

- Capture script: /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/capture_ntm_tui.sh
- Suite runner: /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/run_ntm_tui_suite.sh

## Overarching Goals
1. Replace hand-rolled components with battle-tested Bubbles equivalents
2. Add spring-based physics animations via Harmonica (already a dependency!)
3. Enable mouse interaction for modern terminal UX
4. Decompose the dashboard.go monolith for maintainability
5. Add Huh forms for interactive workflows
6. Verify every change via TUI Inspector capture → compare → iterate loop
7.  {"id":"br-pihrr","title":"Phase 2b: Add bubbles/table to History and Timeline Panels","description":"# Add bubbles/table to History and Timeline Panels

## Background
panels/history.go (862 LOC) renders tabular data with manual string formatting AND has
3 filter systems: historyStatusFilter (all/ok/error), historyAgentFilter (all/claude/codex/
gemini), historyTimeFilter (all/1h/24h/7d). It also has cursor management, replay on Enter,
and keybindings for filter cycling.

panels/timeline.go (1,111 LOC) similarly renders events with agent colors.

bubbles/table provides column alignment, scrolling, selection, and mouse support — but we
must PRESERVE the existing filter systems and replay functionality.

## CRITICAL: Existing Filter Architecture
  historyStatusFilter: Cycle with 'f' key → all/ok/error
  historyAgentFilter: Cycle with 'a' key → all/claude/codex/gemini
  historyTimeFilter: Cycle with 't' key → all/1h/24h/7d
  applyFilters(): Combines all 3 filters → produces filtered entry list
  moveCursor(): Manages selection within filtered results

bubbles/table does NOT have built-in filtering. The filter logic STAYS in the panel.
Only the RENDERING of filtered results moves to bubbles/table.

## Implementation Plan — History Panel

### 1. Add table.Model to HistoryPanel struct
  type HistoryPanel struct {
      PanelBase
      table    table.Model        // NEW: replaces inline rendering
      entries  []history.Entry    // raw data
      filtered []history.Entry    // after filters applied
      // ... existing filter fields preserved
  }

### 2. Define columns (adaptive to tier)
  func historyColumns(width int) []table.Column {
      if width < 120 { return narrowColumns() }
      if width < 200 { return splitColumns() }
      return wideColumns()
  }

### 3. Convert filtered entries → table.Row on each filter change
  func (m *HistoryPanel) rebuildTableRows() {
      rows := make([]table.Row, len(m.filtered))
      for i, e := range m.filtered {
          rows[i] = table.Row{formatTime(e.Timestamp), e.Command, e.Target, statusBadge(e.Status)}
      }
      m.table.SetRows(rows)
  }

### 4. Filter cycling preserved — after filter change, call rebuildTableRows()
### 5. Replay on Enter — get selected row index, map back to filtered[index]
### 6. Style with Catppuccin theme
### 7. Route table.Update(msg) when panel focused

## Timeline Panel — Similar approach
  Columns: Time, Event Type, Agent, Description
  Agent column uses AgentBadge for color coding
  No filter system (simpler than history)

## Unit Tests (REQUIRED)

### Expand existing: panels/history_test.go (634 LOC)
  func TestHistoryTableColumns(t *testing.T) {
      p := NewHistoryPanel()
      p.SetSize(120, 20)
      p.SetEntries(testHistoryEntries(10), nil)
      view := p.View()
      if !strings.Contains(view, "Time") { t.Error("missing Time header") }
      if !strings.Contains(view, "Command") { t.Error("missing Command header") }
  }

  func TestHistoryTableNavigation(t *testing.T) {
      p := NewHistoryPanel()
      p.SetSize(120, 20)
      p.SetEntries(testHistoryEntries(5), nil)
      p.Focus()
      p.Update(tea.KeyMsg{Type: tea.KeyDown})
      sel, ok := p.selectedEntry()
      if !ok { t.Error("should have selection after down") }

 {"id":"br-rb9oj","title":"Overlay-Feed T9: Integration tests and Go e2e scenarios with artifact logging for overlay-feed interaction","description":"# Why this bead exists
Once the attention feed is operational and the overlay integration beads are implemented, we need integration and end-to-end verification that proves the human/agent handoff loop actually works. The earlier version of this bead planned shell-script e2e drivers, which is a poor fit for this Go-only repo. This bead now standardizes on Go-based integration and e2e harnesses with detailed artifact logging.

# Scope
1. Add in-process Go integration tests for overlay/feed interaction, including:
   - attention panel data flow and truncation
   - badge state transitions
   - cursor propagation through zoom/dismiss continuity paths
   - attention-cursor startup/focus behavior
   - graceful degradation when feed visibility is unavailable
2. Add Go-based e2e scenarios under `e2e/` that drive real tmux sessions for:
   - overlay launch and dismiss
   - auto-popup detection
   - overlay binding setup and cleanup
   - zoom-to-pane with cursor hint propagation
   - `assign --watch` overlay discoverability
   - robot-initiated overlay handoff once available
3. Make the Go e2e harness idempotent and self-cleaning so it creates and tears down its own tmux sessions/resources.
4. Ensure all scenarios use the shared Go scenario/artifact harness instead of inventing separate fixture and logging conventions.
5. Ensure all scenarios produce detailed, timestamped artifacts in predictable locations inside the repo's test artifact structure or an explicitly documented temporary artifact root.
6. Keep graceful degradation scenarios first-class rather than treating them as an afterthought.
7. Use Go test code as the canonical harness. Auxiliary debugging helpers are fine, but not shell-script primary runners.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Subtest-based integration coverage with `t.Logf` diagnostics for state transitions, cursor propagation, and rendered output.
- Go e2e scenarios with timeout protection, explicit cleanup, and artifact capture for stdout/stderr, key state transitions, and session lifecycle.
- Artifact logs should include timestamps, session identifiers, cursor values, focus targets, and degraded-mode markers where relevant.
- Failures should report enough structured context that a maintainer can understand what broke without immediately reproducing it interactively.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Integration coverage exists for the key overlay/feed interaction paths and graceful degradation.
- [ ] Go-based e2e scenarios exist for the main overlay/feed workflows and clean up their own resources.
- [ ] The scenarios reuse the shared Go scenario/artifact harness rather than duplicating fixture plumbing.
- [ ] Detailed timestamped artifacts are produced in predictable locations.
- [ ] The primary verification harness is implemented in Go rather than shell scripts.
- [ ] The bead preserves all previously planned happy-path and degraded-path coverage.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:14:52.872086749Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T01:25:35.949568520Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","e2e","overlay","phase-3","tests","verification"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-5u235","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:08.534278552Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-7746h","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:25:31.544052650Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-7h8it","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:25:31.803751872Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-a6cmp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:13.562886901Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-abp0s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:10.690138392Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-ht8jy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:11.494253800Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-la5zy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:12.350680821Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-ut0d8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:09.720207583Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-x66gc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:14.447924217Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-xq4b0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:07.739874585Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-rkh17","title":"Attention Feed Phase 4a1: Document the operator loop in robot help and capabilities output","description":"# Why this bead exists
Help and capabilities output are the canonical contract surface for both human users and agent drivers. They should not be bundled with dashboard rendering concerns. This bead isolates the machine-readable and CLI-discoverability side of the work.

# Scope
1. Update `--robot-help` and `--robot-capabilities` so the attention-feed command cluster, condition taxonomy, cursor/resync behavior, and profile/preset system are explicitly documented.
2. Include concrete examples for the canonical operator loop: snapshot bootstrap, steady-state attention/digest, raw event replay, and expired-cursor resync.
3. Make unsupported or partially observable conditions explicit in capabilities output, especially for `bead_orphaned` if it remains unavailable.
4. Ensure examples and schema notes are accurate enough that future users do not need a separate markdown plan document.
5. Keep the output aligned with actual implementation rather than aspirational language.
6. Expose a machine-readable support matrix covering commands, conditions, profiles, transport features, degraded/unsupported reasons, and any schema/capability version markers needed by agent clients.
7. Document cursor-retention and transport-liveness expectations strongly enough that long-lived clients can adapt without trial and error.

# Design considerations
- Help/capabilities are part of the product surface, not an afterthought.
- Capability output should be precise enough for an agent to adapt automatically.
- The text should emphasize the one-obvious-way operator loop rather than listing an undifferentiated pile of commands.
- Machine-readable capability output should tell agents not just what exists, but which features are supported, degraded, or intentionally unavailable and why.

# Likely touch points
- `internal/cli/root.go`
- capability/help generation in robot-mode code
- `internal/output/`

# Detailed verification expectations
- Golden tests for help/capabilities output.
- Tests proving the documented conditions, profiles, commands, schema-version markers, and transport-feature support matrix match implementation reality.
- Diff-friendly diagnostics when examples or capability flags drift.
- Scenario checks that help/capabilities mention snapshot bootstrap, resync, retention hints, and transport-liveness expectations explicitly.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Help and capabilities output accurately teach the operator loop.
- [ ] Conditions, profiles, transport features, and unsupported/degraded behavior are represented honestly.
- [ ] Capability output is machine-readable enough for agent clients to adapt automatically.
- [ ] The contract is discoverable from ntm itself without external documents.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":180,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:21.584305811Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T01:25:34.736588130Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","dashboard","docs","phase-4","robot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-6tzh9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:27.565570681Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-7kigk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:27.950976700Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-91gti","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:28.696168421Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-kpvhy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:26.873210361Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-slg9g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:57:17.501881264Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-t540i","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:28.315242931Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-tsqxo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:27.204026005Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"br-slg9g","title":"Attention Feed Phase 2a2: Add snapshot attention summary and bootstrap ergonomics","description":"# Why this bead exists
Once snapshot bootstrap correctness exists, snapshot still needs to be genuinely useful to operators. A raw cold-start dump with a cursor is functional, but not ergonomic. This bead adds the snapshot-level attention summary and bootstrap UX polish that helps a frontier LLM orient quickly without turning snapshot into a second digest implementation.

# Scope
1. Add a compact snapshot-level attention summary aligned with the same actionability/severity taxonomy used by the feed and digest surfaces.
2. Surface top attention items or compact counts in a way that helps an operator choose the next command without rereading the entire snapshot.
3. Ensure any snapshot summary stays consistent with the real signal set: core signals, mail signals, conflict signals, and honest unsupported-condition behavior.
4. Add resync guidance and next-step hints that are mechanical rather than speculative.
5. Keep snapshot summary logic shallow enough that digest remains the canonical steady-state delta surface.

# Design considerations
- Snapshot should help an operator orient, not replace the digest.
- Summary counts and hints must be generated from the same underlying signal taxonomy so snapshot/digest do not drift apart.
- Unsupported or partially observable conditions must be represented honestly, not smoothed over.

# Likely touch points
- `internal/robot/robot.go`
- snapshot-producing helpers
- `internal/output/`
- help/capabilities examples that describe bootstrap flow

# Detailed verification expectations
- Golden tests for snapshot responses that include attention summary fields.
- Tests that compare snapshot summary buckets against the underlying signal state for consistency.
- Transcript scenarios showing a realistic cold-start operator loop using the summary to choose the next targeted command.
- Detailed logs in tests for summary bucket counts, unsupported-condition markers, and next-step hints.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Snapshot surfaces a compact operator-oriented summary without duplicating digest semantics.
- [ ] Snapshot summary is derived from the same real signal taxonomy as the rest of the attention-feed system.
- [ ] Unsupported or partial conditions are represented honestly.
- [ ] Bootstrap ergonomics are materially better for users while preserving the cold-start contract.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":150,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:57:12.030218053Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:44:39.255932737Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T08:44:39.255432441Z","close_reason":"SnapshotAttentionSummary type and buildSnapshotAttentionSummary function added. -{"id":"br-t540i","title":"Attention Feed Phase 3d: Add --robot-attention as the one obvious tending primitive","description":"# Why this bead exists
An agent-friendly system should have one obvious tending primitive, not five equally plausible loops. This bead adds `--robot-attention` as the recommended macro for operator agents: sleep until attention is needed, then wake with a compact digest.

# Scope
1. Implement `--robot-attention` as a transparent composition of wait + digest over the same cursor model.
2. Return the wake reason / matched condition alongside the digest so the operator knows why it woke up.
3. Preserve full transparency: no hidden planning, no hidden state machine, no magic side effects.
4. Make cursor handoff obvious so a tending loop can call the command repeatedly without bespoke bookkeeping.
5. Ensure the command works cleanly starting from a snapshot-provided cursor and after an expired-cursor resync.

# Design considerations
- This should be the one command a fungible tending agent can memorize first.
- The command should reduce token burn, not add ceremony.
- It must remain a convenience surface, not a new coordinator.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Tests for wake-on-attention behavior.
- Tests for cursor chaining across repeated calls.
- Golden tests for the combined response shape.
- Transcript-style looping scenario that runs snapshot -> attention -> targeted follow-up -> attention again, with a simulated replay-expiration/resync hop.
- Detailed logs for wake reason, returned digest summary, and next cursor when test expectations fail.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `--robot-attention` exists and clearly behaves like wait-then-digest.
- [ ] Wake reason / matched condition is explicit in the response.
- [ ] Cursor chaining is obvious enough for a long-lived tending loop.
- [ ] The command introduces no hidden orchestration state.
- [ ] Snapshot/resync interactions are covered by automated scenario tests.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":90,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:21.367086542Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T00:57:15.037223892Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention","attention-feed","cli","phase-3","robot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-t540i","depends_on_id":"br-6tzh9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:26.532342527Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-t540i","depends_on_id":"br-7kigk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:26.188583019Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"br-tsqxo","title":"Attention Feed Phase 2c: Upgrade SSE, WebSocket, and HTTP transport to expose the same feed","description":"# Why this bead exists
The CLI cannot be the only way to consume the attention feed. ntm already has SSE, WebSocket, and HTTP surfaces, but they currently expose only a small subset of what an operator agent actually needs. This bead upgrades network transport so remote or embedded clients see the same feed contract instead of a watered-down variant.

# Scope
1. Upgrade the existing event streaming surfaces so they emit the normalized envelope rather than minimal type/timestamp/session stubs.
2. Add replay and filter support where appropriate so network clients can resume from a cursor instead of polling snapshots.
3. Preserve parity between CLI and network outputs; the same event should look materially the same regardless of transport.
4. Keep transport-specific wrappers thin. The feed contract should live in shared code, not in per-endpoint ad hoc shaping.
5. Make replay failure / cursor-expiration semantics as explicit over the network as they are in CLI mode.
6. Define explicit liveness behavior for long-lived transports: heartbeat/keepalive strategy, idle-period semantics, and reconnect guidance so clients can distinguish a quiet stream from a dead connection.
7. Ensure machine-readable capability/version output can tell remote clients which transport features are actually supported.

# Design considerations
- SSE and WebSocket differ operationally, but they should not invent different semantics.
- If replay/resume cannot be satisfied, the transport should return the same resync guidance the CLI gets.
- This bead should improve agent ergonomics for remote controllers without forcing them to shell out to the CLI.
- Heartbeats should be useful enough for liveness detection without creating pointless noise or a second event taxonomy.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Integration tests for SSE replay/subscribe semantics.
- Integration tests for WebSocket event shape, resume behavior, and idle-period/heartbeat behavior.
- Parity tests comparing CLI/network payload shape for the same underlying event.
- Go-based e2e scenarios that exercise HTTP/SSE/WebSocket flows and write detailed transcript logs for initial subscribe, replay, idle heartbeat, expiration, reconnect, and resync behavior.
- Failure logs that capture transport, cursor, filter set, liveness behavior, and payload summary when parity tests fail.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Network clients can subscribe to the normalized feed with the same core fields and cursor semantics as CLI clients.
- [ ] Replay/resume behavior is explicit and consistent.
- [ ] Existing `/events` / WebSocket behavior is upgraded without creating a second schema family.
- [ ] Long-lived transport liveness/heartbeat semantics are explicit and tested.
- [ ] Network parity is demonstrated by automated tests and transcript logs.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":180,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:20.476009644Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T01:25:32.683813492Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","phase-2","robot","server","streaming"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-tsqxo","depends_on_id":"br-04qv0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:23.842024051Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-tsqxo","depends_on_id":"br-kpvhy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:27.712423871Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

+{"id":"br-tsqxo","title":"Attention Feed Phase 2c: Upgrade SSE, WebSocket, and HTTP transport to expose the same feed","description":"# Why this bead exists
The CLI cannot be the only way to consume the attention feed. ntm already has SSE, WebSocket, and HTTP surfaces, but they currently expose only a small subset of what an operator agent actually needs. This bead upgrades network transport so remote or embedded clients see the same feed contract instead of a watered-down variant.

# Scope
1. Upgrade the existing event streaming surfaces so they emit the normalized envelope rather than minimal type/timestamp/session stubs.
2. Add replay and filter support where appropriate so network clients can resume from a cursor instead of polling snapshots.
3. Preserve parity between CLI and network outputs; the same event should look materially the same regardless of transport.
4. Keep transport-specific wrappers thin. The feed contract should live in shared code, not in per-endpoint ad hoc shaping.
5. Make replay failure / cursor-expiration semantics as explicit over the network as they are in CLI mode.
6. Define explicit liveness behavior for long-lived transports: heartbeat/keepalive strategy, idle-period semantics, and reconnect guidance so clients can distinguish a quiet stream from a dead connection.
7. Ensure machine-readable capability/version output can tell remote clients which transport features are actually supported.

# Design considerations
- SSE and WebSocket differ operationally, but they should not invent different semantics.
- If replay/resume cannot be satisfied, the transport should return the same resync guidance the CLI gets.
- This bead should improve agent ergonomics for remote controllers without forcing them to shell out to the CLI.
- Heartbeats should be useful enough for liveness detection without creating pointless noise or a second event taxonomy.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Integration tests for SSE replay/subscribe semantics.
- Integration tests for WebSocket event shape, resume behavior, and idle-period/heartbeat behavior.
- Parity tests comparing CLI/network payload shape for the same underlying event.
- Go-based e2e scenarios that exercise HTTP/SSE/WebSocket flows and write detailed transcript logs for initial subscribe, replay, idle heartbeat, expiration, reconnect, and resync behavior.
- Failure logs that capture transport, cursor, filter set, liveness behavior, and payload summary when parity tests fail.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Network clients can subscribe to the normalized feed with the same core fields and cursor semantics as CLI clients.
- [ ] Replay/resume behavior is explicit and consistent.
- [ ] Existing `/events` / WebSocket behavior is upgraded without creating a second schema family.
- [ ] Long-lived transport liveness/heartbeat semantics are explicit and tested.
- [ ] Network parity is demonstrated by automated tests and transcript logs.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":180,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:20.476009644Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:52:46.511073239Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T08:52:46.509782812Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","phase-2","robot","server","streaming"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-tsqxo","depends_on_id":"br-04qv0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:23.842024051Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-tsqxo","depends_on_id":"br-kpvhy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:27.712423871Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-ut0d8","title":"Overlay-Feed T2: Make overlay badge and stats bar reflect attention state","description":"# Why this bead exists
Once the overlay can show attention items, the surrounding chrome should also reflect whether anything needs the human right now. A static overlay badge wastes that opportunity. This bead makes the overlay's status bar and affordances attention-aware while still deriving everything from the same underlying summary model.

# Scope
1. Make the overlay badge color and text reflect current attention state rather than acting as a purely decorative popup marker.
2. Add compact attention counts to the stats bar when meaningful items exist, using the same underlying summary state as the attention panel.
3. Add or adapt navigation hints so the human can discover the attention panel quickly when it matters.
4. Keep zero-attention states visually calm and avoid turning the chrome into a noisy alert wall.
5. Respect the same profile/degraded semantics as the rest of the summary surfaces so the badge never disagrees with the panel it points to.

# Design considerations
- Chrome is tiny; every glyph and count must earn its place.
- Badge state should be a pure function of the underlying summary model, not a separately maintained counter.
- Popup-only affordances should stay popup-only unless there is a compelling reason to broaden them later.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Unit tests for badge state transitions across quiet, interesting, action-required, and degraded cases.
- Golden tests for stats bar and help-hint rendering with and without attention items.
- TUI artifact scenarios that show the overlay chrome changing as attention state changes.
- Detailed logs in tests that record the input summary state, chosen badge style/text, count output, and hint visibility.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Overlay badge and stats bar reflect real attention state rather than a decorative constant.
- [ ] Quiet states remain clean while meaningful attention states are unmistakable.
- [ ] Badge/count/help-hint behavior stays aligned with the shared summary model.
- [ ] Automated tests and artifacts make regressions easy to diagnose.","notes":"TanMaple: tightened overlay attention chrome coverage in dashboard tests (focused-attention hint suppression, degraded-feed overlay badge calm state) and added cursor-aware popup attention zoom coverage. rch/go test verification is currently noisy because the dashboard package vet path reports a stale SelectCursor mismatch while compile-only/test runs are also contending with long-running local go test processes in this repo.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:04:38.786343163Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:15:46.878163593Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T08:15:46.877147799Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","dashboard","overlay","phase-1","tui"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-ut0d8","depends_on_id":"br-5u235","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:10:26.005786547Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-ut0d8","depends_on_id":"br-x66gc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:10:36.286747807Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-vdfjr","title":"Attention Feed Phase 1d2: Add reservation and file conflict attention signals","description":"# Why this bead exists
Reservation conflicts and file conflicts are a different class of coordination problem from mail. They are operational hazards, not conversation events. This bead isolates conflict-oriented coordination signals so they can be tuned and verified separately from mail behavior.

# Scope
1. Surface `reservation_conflict` when ntm can observe meaningful reservation contention that deserves operator review.
2. Surface `file_conflict` when file-level conflict or overlap conditions are observable through ntm's existing integrations and watchers.
3. Attach structured conflict details such as path/pattern, holder identity when available, and suggested next actions.
4. Keep absence semantics honest where observability is partial or integration-dependent.
5. Ensure these signals remain pointers to the real reservation/conflict source of truth rather than becoming a second coordination system.

# Design considerations
- Conflict signals have different urgency and remediation paths from mail and deserve separate tests and thresholds.
- `reservation_conflict` and `file_conflict` should not be collapsed into one generic problem bucket.
- If a conflict cannot be proven from observable data, the system should not emit it.

# Likely touch points
- conflict / watcher / alert integration points already present in ntm
- shared feed/signal logic
- Agent Mail reservation visibility where applicable

# Detailed verification expectations
- Tests for reservation conflict emission and non-emission.
- Tests for file conflict emission and non-emission.
- Tests for partial observability and honest absence behavior.
- Transcript scenarios showing a tending agent waking because of a concrete conflict condition.
- Detailed logs in tests that capture the observed conflict inputs and the exact reason for emitting or suppressing a signal.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `reservation_conflict` and `file_conflict` are implemented as distinct, evidence-backed signals.
- [ ] The signals expose enough detail for targeted follow-up.
- [ ] The system does not fabricate conflicts from incomplete evidence.
- [ ] Conflict behavior is covered by detailed unit/integration/transcript scenarios.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":150,"created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:35.297092174Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:05:07.411504604Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T08:05:07.411053198Z","close_reason":"Added ReservationConflictEvent and FileConflictEvent to events bus, wired into NewBusAttentionEvent with proper category/type/actionability/severity, structured details with conflict_kind/path/holders/agents, NextActions pointing to robot-locks and robot-tail. 8 tests covering normalization, feed integration, partial observability, and JSON roundtrip.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","conflicts","coordination","phase-1","robot","signals"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-vdfjr","depends_on_id":"br-04qv0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:39.825341682Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-x66gc","title":"Epic: Overlay–Attention Feed Integration — Bridge human and agent control surfaces","description":"# Strategic context
ntm is gaining two complementary surfaces for tending sessions:
1. an agent-facing attention feed (`--robot-events`, `--robot-digest`, `--robot-wait`, `--robot-attention`), and
2. human-facing live summaries in the dashboard/overlay family.

If those surfaces evolve independently, the product will feel schizophrenic: the agent will react to one frontier while the human sees another. This epic exists to make the overlay family consume the same attention model rather than inventing its own.

# Architectural position
This epic is subordinate to the core attention-feed work. It must not:
- create a second event taxonomy,
- replace the feed contract,
- introduce a parallel coordination system,
- or make overlay-specific state load-bearing for robot-mode correctness.

It should:
- reuse the same attention-summary/view-model where possible,
- make human attention state visible in the overlay family,
- add thin handoff signals that help human and agent operators avoid stepping on each other,
- and leave behind transcript/TUI artifacts that make regressions diagnosable.

# Child work covered here
- T1: attention items panel in the overlay
- T2: attention-aware badge, stats bar, and navigation hints
- T3: cursor-aware zoom/dismiss continuity events and hints
- T4: open overlay pre-focused on a specific attention cursor
- T5: integrate assign/watch workflow with overlay discoverability
- T6: add a robot-mode overlay handoff surface
- T7: verify the whole overlay/feed integration and record invariants

# Success criteria
- The overlay family surfaces the same meaningful attention frontier the agent-facing system does.
- Human actions in the overlay can be surfaced back into the attention feed honestly and mechanically.
- No overlay integration bead is allowed to fork the core taxonomy or silently duplicate signal logic.
- Each child bead leaves behind good golden/TUI/transcript artifacts so future regressions are obvious.

# Roll-up behavior
This bead is a roll-up epic, not an independently actionable task. It should remain blocked on the final overlay verification gate so `bv` does not surface it as ready work ahead of the actual implementation beads.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:03:52.789134901Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T01:15:55.536735195Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","dashboard","integration","overlay"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-x66gc","depends_on_id":"br-a1ti3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:15:05.763166454Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
